threats.title: Critical TVL Inflation via UNIv3 Aggregate Position Query
threats.hypothesis_description: The UNIv3Connector calculates TVL by querying the Uniswap V3 pool for the aggregate liquidity of ALL positions managed by the NonfungiblePositionManager at a specific tick range, rather than the specific NFT owned by the vault.
threats.investigation_rationale: If the TVL calculation includes liquidity from other users, an attacker can artificially inflate the vault's TVL by providing their own liquidity to the same pool and tick range. This inflates the vault's share price, allowing the attacker to steal funds from other depositors.

proof_of_signal.pattern: bytes32 key = keccak256(abi.encodePacked(positionManager, tL, tU)); (uint128 liquidity,,,) = pool.positions(key);

preconditions: Vault has an active UNIv3 position
Attacker has capital to provide liquidity to the same Uniswap V3 pool and tick range

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Querying pool-level aggregate position instead of NFT-specific liquidity
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a UNIv3 position is active

operational_questions: Does the positionManager aggregate all user liquidity under a single key in the pool?
Can an attacker mint an NFT with the exact same tick range?
validation_requirements: Deploy a mock UNIv3 pool and position manager
Mint two NFTs in the same tick range and verify if TVL calculation includes both

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Identify an active UNIv3 position held by the vault
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Mint a new UNIv3 NFT via the position manager in the exact same pool and tick range using a flash loan
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Vault TVL is instantly inflated, increasing the share price
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Withdraw previously deposited vault shares at the inflated price, then remove the UNIv3 liquidity and repay the flash loan
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Atomic execution within a single block

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - stolen funds cannot be recovered

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Medium - requires understanding of UNIv3 math and flash loans

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Large UNIv3 mints followed immediately by vault withdrawals
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Sudden spikes in vault share price
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Flash loan usage interacting with both UNIv3 and the vault

threats.title: Vault Maintainer Locked Out Due to Incorrect Modifier Logic
threats.hypothesis_description: The `onlyVaultMaintainer` modifier uses a logical OR (`||`) instead of AND (`&&`) in its revert condition, requiring the caller to be BOTH the maintainer AND possess the EMERGENCY_ROLE to successfully execute the function.
threats.investigation_rationale: This coding error completely breaks the intended access control model. The maintainer (which is intended to be a timelock contract) will be unable to add connectors, update trusted tokens, or remove trusted positions unless it is also granted the emergency role.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (msg.sender != vaults[_vaultId].maintainer || hasRole(EMERGENCY_ROLE, msg.sender) == false) { revert UnauthorizedAccess(); }

preconditions: Maintainer attempts to call a restricted function
Maintainer address does not hold the EMERGENCY_ROLE

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Modifier logic requires two distinct roles simultaneously
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent until contract is upgraded or roles are merged

operational_questions: Is the maintainer address ever granted the EMERGENCY_ROLE in deployment scripts?
Can the protocol function without these administrative capabilities?
validation_requirements: Attempt to call addConnector as the maintainer without EMERGENCY_ROLE
Verify the transaction reverts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Not an external attack, but an operational failure
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Maintainer attempts to execute a timelocked configuration change

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Loss of administrative control over vault connectors
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Inability to upgrade or patch vault integrations
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires Governor to grant EMERGENCY_ROLE to the maintainer

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed transactions from the maintainer address

threats.title: Position Registry Corruption via Mismatched Mapping Keys
threats.hypothesis_description: When removing a holding position, the `updateHoldingPosition` function uses a swap-and-pop mechanism. It reconstructs the mapping key for the moved element using `calculatorConnector`. However, the original key was created using `msg.sender`. For token positions, these addresses differ, causing the mapping to become corrupted.
threats.investigation_rationale: This state corruption will cause `isPositionUsed` to return incorrect indices. Positions may become unremovable, or duplicate positions may be created, eventually exhausting the `maxNumHoldingPositions` limit and causing a Denial of Service.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Creation uses keccak256(abi.encode(msg.sender, ...)); Removal reconstruction uses keccak256(abi.encode(vault.holdingPositions[positionIndex].calculatorConnector, ...))

preconditions: A token position (where calculatorConnector != msg.sender) exists in the holding positions array
Another position is removed, triggering a swap-and-pop that moves the token position

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Inconsistent key generation between mapping insertion and mapping update
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a position is removed

operational_questions: Does the calculatorConnector differ from msg.sender for token positions?
What happens when getHoldingPositionIndex is called after corruption?
validation_requirements: Create a token position and a dummy position
Remove the dummy position to trigger swap-and-pop on the token position
Verify that the token position's index mapping is corrupted

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker observes normal protocol operations adding and removing positions
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: State corruption occurs naturally during normal operations
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker can intentionally trigger removals to accelerate corruption
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Vault reaches maxNumHoldingPositions due to duplicate ghost entries, freezing operations

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Complete DoS of vault deposits and rebalancing
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires contract upgrade or complex state manipulation
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All connector operations that update positions

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Can be triggered at any time

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed position updates, array length approaching maximum
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Connectors unable to add or remove liquidity

threats.title: Complete Bypass of Watcher Security Controls
threats.hypothesis_description: The `Watchers.verifyRemoveLiquidity` function is completely empty. `BaseConnector.sendTokensToTrustedAddress` relies on this function to validate that the accounting manager is not extracting unauthorized token amounts.
threats.investigation_rationale: Because the function contains no logic, any withdrawal amount requested by the accounting manager is automatically approved. This completely neutralizes the intended Watcher security layer, which is supposed to monitor and revert suspicious activity.

proof_of_signal.pattern: function verifyRemoveLiquidity(uint256 withdrawAmount, uint256 sentAmount, bytes memory data) external view { }

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Empty function body used for critical security validation

operational_questions: Was the watcher logic intended to be implemented off-chain?
Can a compromised accounting manager drain all connectors?
validation_requirements: Verify that sendTokensToTrustedAddress succeeds regardless of the amounts passed

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker compromises the accounting manager or finds a bug in its withdrawal logic
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Accounting manager requests a massive withdrawal from a connector

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Potential total loss of funds if primary controls fail

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Unusually large withdrawals bypassing normal limits
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Watcher contract emitting no events or reverts

threats.title: Disabled Swap and Bridge Routes Remain Executable
threats.hypothesis_description: The `onlyExistingRoute` modifier in `SwapAndBridgeHandler` uses a logical AND (`&&`) incorrectly. It only reverts if the route address is zero AND the route is disabled. A disabled route with a non-zero address will pass the check.
threats.investigation_rationale: If a specific bridge or swap route is compromised (e.g., LiFi vulnerability), the maintainer's attempt to disable it via `setEnableRoute(routeId, false)` will be ineffective. Eligible users and Keepers can still execute transactions through the compromised route.
preconditions: A route is explicitly disabled by the maintainer
The route's address is not address(0)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Incorrect boolean logic in access control modifier
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During an active incident where a route is disabled

operational_questions: Are routes ever set to address(0) when disabled?
Can Keepers be tricked into using a disabled route?
validation_requirements: Disable a route using setEnableRoute
Attempt to execute a swap using the disabled route
Verify the swap succeeds

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: A vulnerability is discovered in an external bridge (e.g., LiFi)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Protocol maintainers disable the route to protect funds
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker or compromised Keeper continues to route funds through the disabled route
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Funds are lost to the external vulnerability despite administrative action

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Loss of funds during external protocol exploits

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Transactions using routes where isEnabled == false

threats.title: Dolomite Borrow Positions Are Incorrectly Removed from Registry
threats.hypothesis_description: When opening a new borrow position in `DolomiteConnector.openBorrowPosition`, the contract calls `registry.updateHoldingPosition` with the `removePosition` flag set to `true` instead of `false`.
threats.investigation_rationale: This coding error means the newly created borrow position is immediately removed from the registry's tracking. The debt will not be included in TVL calculations, leading to an artificially inflated vault TVL and incorrect share pricing.
preconditions: Keeper executes openBorrowPosition on the Dolomite connector

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Passing 'true' to a removal flag when opening a position
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a Dolomite borrow is opened

operational_questions: Is the debt tracked anywhere else?
Does the TVL calculation rely strictly on the registry holding positions?
validation_requirements: Open a borrow position in Dolomite
Check the registry holding positions array
Verify the position is missing

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Keeper opens a borrow position in Dolomite as part of a strategy
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: The position is not recorded in the registry
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Vault TVL is calculated without subtracting the Dolomite debt
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Share price inflates; existing users can withdraw more than their fair share

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Inaccurate accounting and potential loss of funds

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Dolomite borrow events without corresponding registry additions

threats.title: Incorrect TVL Calculation for Non-18 Decimal Tokens in Silo
threats.hypothesis_description: The `SiloConnector._getPositionTVL` function hardcodes `1e18` when fetching prices from the oracle and normalizing amounts. It assumes all tokens in the Silo pool have 18 decimals.
threats.investigation_rationale: If the vault interacts with a Silo pool containing USDC or USDT (6 decimals), the oracle call will either revert or return a massively incorrect price. This will completely break the vault's TVL calculation, either causing a DoS or allowing severe share price manipulation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: uint256 price = _getValue(assets[i], base, 1e18); totalDepositAmount += depositAmount * price / 1e18;

preconditions: Vault uses SiloConnector with a non-18 decimal token (e.g., USDC)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Hardcoded 1e18 used for token amount scaling regardless of actual token decimals
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Permanent while non-18 decimal tokens are used

operational_questions: Does the value oracle support querying 1e18 units of a 6-decimal token?
Are USDC/USDT planned to be used in Silo strategies?
validation_requirements: Deploy SiloConnector with USDC
Deposit USDC and check TVL calculation
Verify if it reverts or returns an incorrect value

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If oracle reverts, vault is DoS'd. If oracle returns price for 10^12 actual tokens, TVL is massively inflated
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker exploits inflated TVL to drain vault

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Vault DoS or total loss of funds

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverted TVL queries or massive TVL spikes

threats.title: Native ETH Trapped During Swap and Bridge Operations
threats.hypothesis_description: The `executeSwap` and `executeBridge` functions in `SwapAndBridgeHandler` are marked `payable`, allowing users to send native ETH. However, they make external calls to the implementation route without forwarding `msg.value`.
threats.investigation_rationale: Any native ETH sent to these functions will be permanently trapped in the `SwapAndBridgeHandler` contract, as there is no mechanism to forward it to the router or refund it to the user.
preconditions: Eligible user or Keeper sends native ETH to executeSwap or executeBridge

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Payable function does not forward or utilize msg.value
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a native ETH swap is attempted

operational_questions: Is native ETH intended to be swapped directly?
Does the LiFi implementation pull WETH instead?
validation_requirements: Send a transaction to executeSwap with msg.value > 0
Verify that the ETH remains in the SwapAndBridgeHandler contract

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: User or Keeper initiates a swap involving native ETH
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Transaction succeeds, but ETH is not forwarded to LiFi
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Swap fails at the LiFi level due to missing funds, or succeeds using ERC20 tokens while trapping the ETH

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - no rescue function exists in SwapAndBridgeHandler

threats.title: Systemic Vault DoS via TVL Underflow on Underwater Positions
threats.hypothesis_description: Multiple lending connectors (Silo, Prisma, Dolomite, Compound, Aave) calculate TVL using unchecked subtraction (`collateral - debt`). In Solidity 0.8.x, this reverts if debt exceeds collateral.
threats.investigation_rationale: Because `TVLHelper.getTVL` iterates over all positions, a single underwater position will cause the entire vault's TVL calculation to revert. This freezes all deposits, withdrawals, and management operations indefinitely.
preconditions: Vault has an active borrow position
Market conditions or interest accrual cause debt to exceed collateral

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Unsafe subtraction in a loop that affects core protocol availability
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During market downturns or liquidation events

operational_questions: Are positions actively managed to prevent liquidation?
What happens if a flash crash occurs?
validation_requirements: Simulate an underwater position in a test environment
Attempt to call getTVL() and verify it reverts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Market volatility causes a vault's collateral value to drop below its debt
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: The specific connector's _getPositionTVL function reverts on underflow
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Users cannot withdraw funds, and Keepers cannot rebalance to fix the position

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - requires emergency intervention or market recovery

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverted transactions interacting with the AccountingManager

threats.title: Keeper Privilege Escalation via Unvalidated Flash Loan Targets
threats.hypothesis_description: In `BalancerFlashLoan.receiveFlashLoan`, the contract executes arbitrary calls to addresses in the `destinationConnector` array without validating that they are trusted connectors. Only the token receiver is validated.
threats.investigation_rationale: A compromised or malicious Keeper can use the flash loan mechanism to execute arbitrary calls to any contract with any calldata. This bypasses all connector-specific safety checks and allows the Keeper to interact with unauthorized protocols or drain funds.
preconditions: Keeper multisig is compromised or acts maliciously
Keeper initiates a flash loan transaction

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Execution of arbitrary calldata on unvalidated target addresses
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time a flash loan is executed

operational_questions: Why are destination connectors not validated against the registry?
Can the flash loan contract's context be used to bypass other security checks?
validation_requirements: Execute a flash loan as a Keeper
Pass an arbitrary external contract as a destinationConnector
Verify the call succeeds

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Attacker submits a flash loan transaction with malicious destination addresses and calldata
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Flash loan contract executes the calls, potentially approving tokens to the attacker or interacting with malicious contracts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker drains funds from the protocol

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Flash loans interacting with unknown contracts

threats.title: Share Price Manipulation via Stale Cross-Chain TVL Updates
threats.hypothesis_description: The `OmnichainManagerBaseChain.updateTVL` function accepts any `updateTime` without checking if it is newer than the existing timestamp. It blindly updates the registry with the provided TVL and timestamp.
threats.investigation_rationale: If LayerZero messages are delayed or delivered out of order, an older TVL value can overwrite a newer one. An attacker observing this could exploit the temporary mispricing to buy cheap shares or withdraw at an inflated price.
preconditions: LayerZero message delivery is delayed or reordered

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Lack of monotonicity check on timestamped data updates
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: During network congestion or relayer issues

operational_questions: Does LayerZero guarantee ordered delivery for this specific OApp configuration?
How much can the TVL fluctuate between updates?
validation_requirements: Send two TVL updates out of order
Verify that the older update successfully overwrites the newer one

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Attacker monitors LayerZero mempool for delayed TVL updates
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: A stale message with a lower TVL is about to be delivered, overwriting a higher current TVL
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Attacker deposits funds right after the stale update, receiving shares at a discount
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: A new update corrects the TVL, and the attacker withdraws for a profit
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires precise timing and network conditions

potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - extracted value cannot be recovered

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Deposits/withdrawals perfectly timed around cross-chain TVL updates
